Summary of Cybersecurity Article: Webworm Deploys Custom Backdoors Using Discord and MS Graph API
Cybersecurity researchers have identified new activity from the China-aligned threat actor Webworm, which has been active since at least 2022. Webworm has been targeting government agencies and enterprises in various sectors, including IT services, aerospace, and electric power, in countries such as Russia, Georgia, Mongolia, and several other Asian nations.
New Backdoors: EchoCreep and GraphWorm
Webworm has added two new custom backdoors to its toolkit: EchoCreep and GraphWorm. EchoCreep uses Discord for command-and-control (C2) communications, while GraphWorm uses Microsoft Graph API. These backdoors allow for file upload/download, command execution, and other malicious activities.
Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs)
Webworm’s TTPs include:
Using GitHub repositories to host malware and tools, such as SoftEther VPN. Leveraging open-source utilities like dirsearch and nuclei to brute-force victim web server files and directories. Employing custom proxy tools, such as WormFrp, ChainWorm, SmuxProxy, and WormSocket, to encrypt communications and chain across multiple hosts. Utilizing Discord and Microsoft Graph API for C2 communications. Overlaps with Other Threat Actors
Webworm’s links to other threat actors, such as Space Pirates, are tenuous at best, with the use of open-source RATs being the primary connection.
Implications
The discovery of EchoCreep and GraphWorm marks an expansion of Webworm’s arsenal, highlighting the group’s continued evolution and adaptation. The use of custom backdoors and proxy tools demonstrates Webworm’s efforts to remain stealthy and evade detection.
Recommendations
Organizations should be aware of Webworm’s TTPs and take measures to prevent and detect these types of attacks, including:
Monitoring for suspicious activity on Discord and Microsoft Graph API. Implementing security controls to prevent brute-force attacks and unauthorized access. Using threat intelligence to stay informed about Webworm’s evolving TTPs. By understanding Webworm’s tactics and techniques, organizations can better protect themselves against these types of cyber threats.
A man is not where he lives but where he loves.
Source: Webworm Deploys EchoCreep and GraphWorm Backdoors Using Discord and MS Graph API

content:
GitHub Breach: Employee Device Hack Leads to Exfiltration of 3,800+ Internal Repos
GitHub, a Microsoft-owned subsidiary, has announced that it is investigating an unauthorized access to its internal repositories after a threat actor known as TeamPCP listed the platform’s source code and internal organizations for sale on a cybercrime forum. The breach occurred due to a compromised employee device, which was infected with a poisoned Microsoft Visual Studio Code extension.
Key Findings:
Exfiltration of internal repositories: The attacker exfiltrated approximately 3,800 internal GitHub repositories, which may include sensitive information such as source code, internal tools, and security protocols. No customer data affected: GitHub claims that there is no evidence of impact to customer information stored outside of its internal repositories. Poisoned VS Code extension: The breach occurred due to a compromised employee device, which was infected with a poisoned Microsoft Visual Studio Code extension. TeamPCP’s involvement: The threat actor TeamPCP is behind the breach and has listed GitHub’s source code for sale on a cybercrime forum for a minimum of $50,000. LAPSUS$ cybercrime group involvement: The LAPSUS$ cybercrime group has teamed up with TeamPCP to jointly sell GitHub repositories for $95,000. Additional Incidents:
Durabletask PyPI package compromise: TeamPCP has compromised the durabletask PyPI package, which is an official Microsoft Python client for the Durable Task workflow execution framework. Mini Shai-Hulud malware campaign: TeamPCP’s self-replicating malware campaign, known as Mini Shai-Hulud, continues to expand in reach, with the compromise of the durabletask package being the latest incident. Recommendations:
Monitor infrastructure: GitHub is closely monitoring its infrastructure for follow-on activity and will notify customers via established incident response and notification channels if any impact is discovered. Rotate critical secrets: GitHub has rotated critical secrets as a risk mitigation measure. Prioritize highest-impact credentials: GitHub is prioritizing highest-impact credentials to minimize potential damage. Overall, the breach highlights the importance of robust security measures, including employee device security, to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive information.
I find hope in the darkest of days, and focus in the brightest. I do not judge the universe. Author: Dalai Lama
Source: GitHub Breached — Employee Device Hack Led to Exfiltration of 3,800+ Internal Repos

content:
The article discusses a vulnerability in ExifTool, a widely used utility for reading and writing metadata in image, PDF, audio, and video files. The vulnerability, identified as CVE-2026-3102, was discovered by Kaspersky’s Global Research and Analysis Team in February 2026 and patched by the developers within the same month.
Vulnerability Details:
The vulnerability affects macOS systems with ExifTool version 13.49 and earlier. It allows an attacker to run arbitrary commands by hiding instructions inside an image file’s metadata. The vulnerability is triggered when the -n flag is used, which outputs machine-readable data without additional processing. The attacker can inject malicious commands into the FileCreateDate metadata field, which is not properly sanitized. Exploitation:
The attacker can use the -tagsFromFile feature to copy a malicious date string into the FileCreateDate field. The malicious date string can contain single quotes, which can be used to break the command structure and execute arbitrary shell commands. The attacker can use the DateTimeOriginal tag as a source tag to copy the malicious date string into the FileCreateDate field. Patch Analysis:
The patch for the vulnerability was released in ExifTool version 13.50. The patch replaces the string concatenation with a list-based system call, which eliminates the need for manual escaping and removes the vulnerability. The patch also creates a new System() wrapper that redirects all I/O to /dev/null and restores them after the call. Protection:
To protect against the vulnerability, users should ensure that all photo processing workflows are using the updated version of ExifTool (13.50 or later). Users should also verify that all asset management platforms, photo organization apps, and bulk image processing scripts running on Macs are calling the updated version of ExifTool. Additional measures, such as using Kaspersky Open Source Software Threats Data Feed and Kaspersky for macOS, can help harden defenses against similar vulnerabilities. Conclusion:
The vulnerability highlights the risks of inconsistent input sanitization in tools that bridge high-level metadata parsing with platform-specific utilities. The vulnerability underscores the danger of manual escaping routines in evolving codebases. The patch provides a robust, architecture-level fix that eliminates shell interpretation risks entirely, and reinforces the core security principle of replacing fragile string concatenation with secure, list Silence is deep as Eternity, Speech is shallow as Time. Author: Carlyle
Source: How an image could compromise your Mac: understanding an ExifTool vulnerability (CVE-2026-3102)

Vulnerability Details:

  • It allows an attacker to run arbitrary commands by hiding instructions inside an image file’s metadata.

content:
The Electronic Privacy Information Center (EPIC) has released a report detailing the deceptive methods used by major data-collecting companies to prevent consumers from opting out of the sale and sharing of their personal information. The report found that 38 major data companies, including Google, Meta, and OpenAI, use manipulative design to make it difficult for users to opt out, with tactics such as burying links in fine print, requiring multiple forms to be completed, and demanding that users create accounts or pay for subscriptions before opting out.
The report highlights the case of people-search data brokers, such as Spokeo, Whitepages, and National Public Data, which do not offer a way for consumers to opt out of the sale or transfer of their data. Instead, these companies provide a process for removing individual listings by URL, one at a time, with no commitment to stop selling the same person’s information in the future. This can have serious safety implications, particularly for individuals such as domestic violence survivors, public officials, and their families, who may need to remove their information from circulation to protect themselves from harm.
EPIC’s researchers found that many companies, including Bumble, default users into data sharing through preselected toggles, while others, such as OpenAI, do not clearly link their opt-out forms from their homepages or privacy policies. Some companies, including Palantir and TikTok, do not provide an option to opt out of the sale or sharing of personal data at all. In response to the report, some companies, such as Amazon and OpenAI, have disputed the findings, claiming that they do not sell customer personal information or that they provide straightforward ways for users to control how their data is used.
The report argues that the opt-out process is often the only mechanism available to remove a home address from circulation before someone shows up at the door, and that companies must design opt-out processes with respect toward consumers’ rights. EPIC concludes that the real remedy is not better forms but less collection, with rules that bar companies from gathering personal information they never needed in the first place. The report’s findings highlight the need for stronger regulations to protect consumer privacy and prevent the misuse of personal data.
The companies implicated in the report have responded in various ways, with some disputing the findings and others acknowledging the need for improved opt-out processes. However, the report’s conclusions emphasize that consumers cannot effectively protect their own privacy by exercising opt-out rights, and that a more fundamental approach is needed to address the issue of data collection and sharing. Ultimately, the report suggests that the only way to truly protect consumer privacy is to limit the amount of personal information that companies are allowed to collect in the first place.
Let go of your attachment to being right, and suddenly your mind is more open. You’re able to benefit from the unique viewpoints of others, without being crippled by your own judgement. Author: Ralph Marston
Source: Data Brokers’ and AI Firms’ Opt-Out Forms Are Built to Fail, Report Finds
summary: The Electronic Privacy Information Center (EPIC) has released a report detailing the deceptive methods used by major data-collecting companies to prevent consumers from opting out of the sale and sharing of their personal information. The report found that 38 major data companies, including Google, Meta, and OpenAI, use manipulative design to make it difficult for users to opt out, with tactics such as burying links in fine print, requiring multiple forms to be completed, and demanding that users create accounts or pay for subscriptions before opting out.
title: Data Brokers’ and AI Firms’ Opt-Out Forms Are Built to Fail, Report Finds

content:
Microsoft Releases Fix for YellowKey BitLocker Bypass Vulnerability
Microsoft has released a mitigation for a zero-day vulnerability known as YellowKey (CVE-2026-45585), which allows attackers to bypass BitLocker encryption on Windows devices. The vulnerability, disclosed by security researcher Chaotic Eclipse, affects Windows 11 and Windows Server 2025, and can be exploited by placing specially crafted files on a USB drive or EFI partition, then rebooting into the Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE) and triggering a shell with unrestricted access.
Impact:
Successful exploitation can allow an attacker with physical access to bypass BitLocker Device Encryption and gain access to encrypted data. Any machine with a USB port and can be rebooted can be a target. Mitigations:
Modify the BootExecute registry value to prevent the FsTx Auto Recovery Utility from starting automatically. Switch from TPM-only to TPM+PIN mode, which requires a PIN to decrypt the drive at startup. Enable the “Require additional authentication at startup” option via Microsoft Intune or Group Policies. Set “Configure TPM startup PIN” to “Require startup PIN with TPM” on devices that are not encrypted. Recommendations:
Apply the mitigation steps to prevent exploitation. Use TPM+PIN mode to add an extra layer of security. Keep devices and software up to date with the latest security patches. By following these mitigations and recommendations, users can protect themselves against the YellowKey vulnerability and prevent unauthorized access to encrypted data.
Giving up doesn’t always mean you are weak; sometimes it means that you are strong enough to let go.
Source: Microsoft Releases Mitigation for YellowKey BitLocker Bypass CVE-2026-45585 Exploit

content:
A cybersecurity breach occurred at Grafana Labs, a company that creates open-source visualization software, on May 11, 2026. The breach was caused by the TanStack npm supply chain attack, which was also responsible for breaches at OpenAI and Mistral AI. The attackers gained access to Grafana’s GitHub environment, including public and private source code, as well as internal operational information and business contact details. However, the company stated that no customer production systems or operations were compromised.
Grafana Labs received an extortion demand from an unnamed threat actor on May 16, but chose not to pay the ransom. In response to the breach, the company has taken steps to enhance its security, including rotating automation tokens, implementing enhanced monitoring, and auditing all commits for signs of malicious activity. The breach is part of a larger investigation into unauthorized access to GitHub’s internal repositories, which is being carried out by the platform.

The breach was caused by the TanStack npm supply chain attack, which was also responsible for breaches at OpenAI and Mistral AI. The attackers gained access to Grafana’s GitHub environment, including public and private source code, as well as internal operational information and business contact details. However, the company stated that no customer production systems or operations were compromised.

A proof-of-concept (PoC) exploit code has been released for a Linux kernel vulnerability known as DirtyDecrypt (CVE-2026-31635), which allows for local privilege escalation (LPE). The vulnerability was discovered by the Zellic and V12 security team and is a variant of previously disclosed vulnerabilities such as Copy Fail, Dirty Frag, and Fragnesia.
The DirtyDecrypt vulnerability is caused by a missing copy-on-write (COW) guard in the rxgk_decrypt_skb function, which allows an attacker to write to the memory of privileged processes or the page cache of privileged files, leading to LPE. The vulnerability only affects Linux distributions with CONFIG_RXGK enabled, such as Fedora, Arch Linux, and openSUSE Tumbleweed.
The release of the PoC code has prompted Linux kernel developers to review a proposal for an emergency “killswitch” that would allow administrators to disable vulnerable kernel functions at runtime until a patch for a zero-day vulnerability becomes available. Additionally, Rocky Linux has introduced an optional security repository that allows for urgent security fixes to be shipped quickly in response to severe vulnerabilities.
The vulnerability has a CVSS score of 7.5 and is considered a significant threat, especially in containerized environments where it could provide a pathway to escape the pod. Users are advised to update their Linux kernels to the latest version and to be cautious when using vulnerable distributions.
Key points:
DirtyDecrypt is a Linux kernel vulnerability that allows for local privilege escalation (LPE) The vulnerability is caused by a missing COW guard in the rxgk_decrypt_skb function It only affects Linux distributions with CONFIG_RXGK enabled A proof-of-concept (PoC) exploit code has been released Linux kernel developers are reviewing a proposal for an emergency “killswitch” to disable vulnerable kernel functions Rocky Linux has introduced an optional security repository for urgent security fixes. Remember that failure is an event, not a person. Author: Zig Ziglar
Source: DirtyDecrypt PoC Released for Linux Kernel CVE-2026-31635 LPE Vulnerability

A new US law, the Take It Down Act, requires tech platforms to provide a way for people to report nonconsensual intimate images and videos (NCII) uploaded to their platforms. The law, which went into effect on May 19, applies to a range of apps and online services, including social media and gaming platforms.
To comply with the law, platforms must make it easy for people to submit removal requests, which must include:
A way to locate the content, such as a link A statement explaining that it was not uploaded consensually A signature from the person depicted or someone authorized to act on their behalf Contact information for the person submitting the request Once a request is submitted, the platform has 48 hours to determine its validity and remove the content if it is deemed nonconsensual. Some platforms, such as Meta, Microsoft, Google, and Reddit, have dedicated forms for submitting takedown requests, while others may use email addresses or third-party tools.
Experts note that the effectiveness of the law will depend on how easily people can find and use the reporting tools, particularly for younger users who may not be aware of their rights or able to navigate complex reporting forms. Some companies, such as Snap and TikTok, have updated their reporting forms to include references to the Take It Down Act, while others, such as X Corp. and Verizon, did not respond to requests for comment.
The law is seen as an important step in protecting individuals from digital harm, particularly in the context of nonconsensual intimate imagery and deepfakes. However, its success will depend on the willingness of tech platforms to comply with the law and make reporting tools accessible and user-friendly.
However many holy words you read, however many you speak, what good will they do you if you do not act on upon them? Author: Buddha

content:
In Q1 2026, Kaspersky products blocked over 343 million attacks originating from various online resources, with Web Anti-Virus responding to 50 million unique links and File Anti-Virus blocking nearly 15 million malicious and potentially unwanted objects. The quarter also saw the detection of 2938 new ransomware variants, with more than 77,000 users experiencing ransomware attacks. Notably, 14% of all ransomware victims whose data was published on threat actors’ data leak sites were victims of the Clop ransomware. Law enforcement agencies made significant strides in combating ransomware, with the FBI seizing the domains of the RAMP cybercrime forum and apprehending individuals linked to the Phobos group.
The most prolific ransomware gangs in Q1 2026 included Clop, Qilin, and The Gentlemen, with Clop returning to the top of the rankings after displacing Qilin. The quarter also saw the emergence of new threat actors, such as The Gentlemen, which had already surpassed the activity levels of established groups like Akira and INC Ransom. In terms of vulnerabilities and attacks, the Interlock group was found to be heavily exploiting a zero-day vulnerability in Cisco Secure FMC firewall management software, demonstrating the ongoing reliance on zero-day vulnerabilities for initial access. The vulnerability enabled arbitrary Java code execution with root privileges on the affected device, highlighting the importance of timely patching and vulnerability management.
The geography of threats varied across different regions, with the United States, the Netherlands, and Germany accounting for the highest proportions of SSH-based attacks on IoT devices. China continued to account for the largest proportion of Telnet attacks, although there was a marked increase in activity originating from Pakistan. In terms of web-based attacks, Kaspersky solutions blocked over 343 million attacks launched from internet resources worldwide, with Web Anti-Virus being triggered by nearly 50 million unique URLs. The countries and territories where users faced the greatest risk of online infection included those with high levels of malicious activity, such as the United States, Russia, and China.
The quarter also saw significant activity in the realm of miners, with Kaspersky solutions detecting 3485 new modifications of miners and protecting over 260,000 unique users from miner attacks. The TOP 10 countries and territories attacked by miners included those with large numbers of Kaspersky users, such as the United States, Russia, and China. In terms of attacks on macOS devices, the quarter saw the discovery of new cryptocurrency theft campaigns and the exploitation of vulnerabilities in iOS and macOS devices. The TOP 20 threats to macOS included a mix of traditional adware and spyware, as well as more specialized threats like the PasivRobber spyware. Overall, the Q1 2026 threat landscape was marked by significant activity across various threat vectors, including ransomware, miners, and web-based attacks.
When you dance, your purpose is not to get to a certain place on the floor. It’s to enjoy each step along the way. Author: Wayne Dyer
Source: IT threat evolution in Q1 2026. Non-mobile statistics

A zero-day exploit against Windows BitLocker has been discovered, allowing attackers to bypass the full-volume encryption protection. This exploit, named YellowKey, was published by a researcher known as Nightmare-Eclipse and can reliably bypass default Windows 11 deployments of BitLocker. BitLocker is a crucial security feature that makes disk contents inaccessible to anyone without the decryption key, which is stored in a trusted platform module (TPM).
The exploit requires physical access to the computer, which limits its potential impact. However, for organizations that rely on BitLocker as a mandatory protection, this vulnerability poses a significant risk. Many organizations, including those that contract with governments, require BitLocker to be enabled on their systems. The fact that YellowKey can bypass this protection means that an attacker with physical access to a computer could potentially access sensitive data.
The publication of the YellowKey exploit has been made available on Nightmare-Eclipse’s GitHub account, and a discussion thread on Slashdot has been started to examine the implications of this vulnerability. The exploit highlights the importance of considering physical security as part of an overall cybersecurity strategy. While BitLocker provides strong protection against unauthorized access, it is not foolproof, and organizations must take additional measures to protect their systems from physical attacks.
The discovery of the YellowKey exploit serves as a reminder that no security system is completely secure, and vulnerabilities can be found in even the most widely used and trusted security features. As a result, organizations must remain vigilant and continually monitor their systems for potential vulnerabilities, while also implementing additional security measures to protect against physical attacks. This includes ensuring that systems are physically secure, using additional authentication mechanisms, and regularly reviewing and updating security protocols.
You can’t let praise or criticism get to you. It’s a weakness to get caught up in either one. Author: John Wooden
Source: Zero-Day Exploit Against Windows BitLocker

The article discusses the current state of cybersecurity, specifically the impact of AI and machine learning on the field. Clive Robinson expresses his concern that the over-reliance on AI and machine learning systems will lead to a decline in human skills and expertise, making it difficult for the West to defend against cyber threats. He argues that current AI systems, including large language models (LLMs), are limited to pattern matching and do not truly reason or think creatively. Robinson believes that the use of AI and machine learning systems will ultimately lead to a loss of technological capability in the West, making it vulnerable to cyber attacks.
Robinson’s comments are in response to a blog post about the decline of the Capture the Flag (CTF) scene, a community of cybersecurity enthusiasts who participate in competitions to test their skills. The author of the post, Kabir, argues that the CTF scene is dying due to a lack of interest and participation from younger generations. Robinson agrees with Kabir’s assessment, but attributes the decline to the over-reliance on AI and machine learning systems, which he believes are stifling human innovation and creativity.
Other commenters, including K.S. and bye bye ai, disagree with Robinson’s assessment of AI and machine learning systems. They argue that LLMs are capable of reasoning and inference, and that they can be used to augment human capabilities. However, Robinson remains skeptical, citing the limitations of current AI systems and the need for human expertise and creativity in cybersecurity. He believes that the West’s reliance on AI and machine learning systems will ultimately lead to a decline in its technological capabilities, making it vulnerable to cyber threats.
The discussion highlights the ongoing debate about the role of AI and machine learning in cybersecurity, and the need for human expertise and creativity in the field. While some argue that AI and machine learning systems can augment human capabilities, others believe that they are limited and may ultimately lead to a decline in human skills and expertise. The conversation underscores the importance of striking a balance between the use of technology and the development of human skills and expertise in cybersecurity.
The article also touches on the idea that the West’s economic activity is increasingly based on non-tangible technology companies that earn income through social media, internet advertising, and surveillance. This has led to a decline in manufacturing and technological capabilities, making the West vulnerable to cyber threats. The discussion highlights the need for a balanced approach to technological development, one that combines the use of AI and machine learning systems with the development of human skills and expertise.
We are the leaves of one branch, the drops of one sea, the flowers of one garden. Author: Jean Lacordaire
Source: Friday Squid Blogging: Bigfin Squid

content:
Some AI-based video age-verification checks can be bypassed using simple methods such as wearing a fake mustache. The primary purpose of these checks is not to verify age, but to de-anonymize critics and enable governments to deny access to online platforms. The failure to keep minors out of adult spaces is not surprising, as this is not the ultimate goal of these checks. Researchers have found that current zero-knowledge proofs have several failings, including the need for interaction, setup, and imperfect soundness.
A recent paper has proposed a new relaxation of zero-knowledge proofs that achieves perfect soundness and no interaction, which could have significant benefits for age verification and other applications. The paper defines a new concept of zero-knowledge that only requires that one cannot rule out the existence of a simulator, rather than requiring that a simulator actually exists. This enables the removal of interaction and setup from classical zero-knowledge proofs, making them more practical and secure.
The discussion around age verification has also touched on the topic of hacking hard drive firmware, with some researchers exploring the possibility of modifying firmware to exploit vulnerabilities. One researcher discovered that some hard drives have backdoor vendor commands and connections to diagnostic ports, which could be used to access and modify the firmware. Others have noted that the microcontrollers in hard drives can be quite powerful and problematic, with some having multiple ARM CPUs and shared memory.
The conversation has also highlighted the importance of security research and the need to stay one step ahead of attackers. As one commenter noted, the question is not whether you can make it difficult for honest users, but whether the system can withstand the creativity of attackers. Another commenter pointed out that the current system can be bypassed by a 12-year-old with a makeup pencil, and that more robust security measures are needed. Researchers are exploring new methods to bypass hardware attestation recapthca, which is being developed by Google, and are looking for ways to cheat the new captcha using Linux desktops or GrapheneOS phones.
The use of Intel ME and AMD PSP has also been discussed, with one researcher noting that these can be removed by formatting the partition in SPI, which can help to improve security by preventing remote access and bypassing system security. However, this requires the right connectors and cables, as well as some technical know-how. Overall, the discussion highlights the ongoing cat-and-mouse game between security researchers and attackers, and the need for continued innovation and vigilance to stay ahead of emerging threats.
A little knowledge that acts is worth infinitely more than much knowledge that is idle. Author: Kahlil Gibran
Source: Bypassing On-Camera Age-Verification Checks

Kimsuky, a Korean-speaking threat actor, has been observed using new malware variants based on the PebbleDash platform, which was originally used by the Lazarus Group. The group’s latest campaigns have shown notable tactical shifts, with the use of VSCode tunneling, Cloudflare Quick Tunnels, DWAgent, large language models, and the Rust programming language. Kimsuky has been targeting organizations in South Korea, as well as in Brazil and Germany, with a focus on the defense sector. The group’s malware, including HelloDoor and httpMalice, is designed to establish backdoors and steal information, and has been distributed through spear-phishing emails and other means.
The PebbleDash cluster, which includes HelloDoor and httpMalice, is considered one of the most technically advanced in Kimsuky’s toolset. These malware variants are designed to evade detection and have been used to target a range of sectors, including defense, government, and medical organizations. HelloDoor, for example, is a Rust-based backdoor that uses a C2 server hosted through TryCloudflare, a temporary tunneling service provided by Cloudflare. httpMalice, on the other hand, is a more recent variant that uses a combination of HTTP and Dropbox communications to interact with its C2 server.
Kimsuky’s use of legitimate tools, such as VSCode and DWAgent, has also been observed. The group uses these tools to establish persistence and conduct post-exploitation activities, such as remote monitoring and management. The group’s ability to adapt and evolve its tactics and tools has made it a significant threat to organizations in the region. The use of large language models to generate code has also been observed, with comments in the code suggesting that it was generated by an LLM service rather than a human developer.
The group’s initial access to target systems is typically gained through spear-phishing emails containing malicious attachments disguised as documents. The attachments often consist of compressed files containing droppers in formats such as JSE, EXE, PIF, or SCR. The droppers are designed to deploy malware, including the PebbleDash and AppleSeed clusters, which are considered the most technically advanced in Kimsuky’s toolset. The group’s ability to use a range of tactics and tools has made it a significant threat to organizations, and its continued evolution and adaptation are likely to pose ongoing challenges to cybersecurity professionals.
Kimsuky’s targeting of South Korean entities, as well as organizations in Brazil and Germany, suggests that the group’s interests are focused on the defense sector and other strategic industries. The group’s use of PebbleDash and AppleSeed malware clusters has been particularly effective in establishing backdoors and stealing information, and its ability to adapt and evolve its tactics and tools has made it a significant threat to organizations in the region. Overall, Kimsuky’s activities pose a significant threat to cybersecurity, and its continued evolution and adaptation are likely to pose ongoing challenges to cybersecurity professionals.

Foxconn is the type of target that is particularly appealing to ransomware and data extortion actors, because it is a massive company with divisions and subsidiaries around the world that hold not only its own intellectual property but that of its customers. The company is a key manufacturing contractor for electronic components or entire devices,
title: Foxconn Ransomware Attack Shows Nothing Is Safe Forever

content:
The ransomware landscape in 2026 is characterized by the emergence of new families adopting post-quantum cryptography ciphers, making it nearly impossible for victims to decrypt their data without paying a ransom. Despite a decline in ransomware attacks, organizations across all sectors continue to face a high likelihood of attack, with the manufacturing sector alone potentially losing over $18 billion in 2025. Ransomware operators are refining their tactics, prioritizing neutralizing endpoint defenses before executing their payloads, and using tools like “EDR killers” to terminate security processes and disable monitoring agents.
The rise of encryptionless extortion attacks is also a notable trend, where attackers focus on extracting sensitive data and leveraging the threat of public disclosure as their primary means of extortion. This shift fundamentally changes the nature of the risk, as backups are no longer effective against data exposure, regulatory consequences, and reputational damage. The industrialization of initial access, with initial access brokers supplying pre-compromised access to corporate environments, has become a critical component of the ransomware ecosystem. The primary access vectors offered for sale include RDP, VPN, and RDWeb, with remote access infrastructure remaining the primary attack surface.
The dark web plays a significant role in the ransomware ecosystem, with Telegram channels and underground forums functioning as platforms for the distribution and sale of compromised datasets and access credentials. Law enforcement agencies have been actively shutting down dark web platforms and ransomware data leak sites, causing inconvenience to ransomware coordination. The top ransomware groups in 2025 included Qilin, Clop, and Akira, with Qilin standing out as one of the fastest-growing and dominant RaaS platforms. New actors in 2026, such as The Gentlemen group, are emerging with more professionalized and controlled operations, reflecting a broader shift toward data-centric extortion strategies.
The Gentlemen group is notable for its emphasis on data-centric extortion strategies, often prioritizing exfiltration and leverage over purely disruptive encryption. This aligns with one of the defining trends of 2026: ransomware evolving into a form of data breach monetization rather than just system denial. Other emerging groups, such as Devman, are also worth noting, as they likely leverage existing tools and infrastructure to launch attacks. The ransomware landscape in 2026 is characterized by increased sophistication, industrialization, and specialization, with attackers adapting to lower ransom payment rates and improved backup practices among victims.
The use of post-quantum cryptography, such as the ML-KEM standard, is becoming more prevalent, with groups like PE32 ransomware family leveraging this technology to secure their AES keys. The Kyber1024 algorithm, used by PE32, provides Level 5 security, roughly equivalent in strength to AES-256, making it nearly impossible for victims to decrypt their data without paying a ransom. As the ransomware landscape continues to evolve, organizations must prioritize preventing initial compromise, detecting misuse of legitimate credentials, and limiting lateral movement within already-breached environments to stay
Love is the master key that opens the gates of happiness. Author: Oliver Holmes
Source: State of ransomware in 2026

content:
The article discusses the dangers of suspicious websites that cannot be definitively classified as phishing but engage in unsafe activities. These sites manipulate users into voluntarily transferring money for non-existent services, signing up for hidden subscriptions, or disclosing personal data. Kaspersky has introduced a new web filtering category, “Sites with an undefined trust level,” to detect such resources.
The article highlights the following key points:
Types of suspicious sites: Fake online stores, dubious crypto exchanges, investment platforms, and services with paid subscriptions are common examples of suspicious sites. Dangers of suspicious sites: Visiting or buying from untrusted websites can lead to financial loss, fraud, and exposure of personal data. Common indicators of suspicious sites: Strange domain names, cheap top-level domains, recently registered domains, unrealistic promises, lack of company contact information, and payments only via cryptocurrency or irreversible bank transfers. Visual and manual clues: Scam sites often have unprofessional visuals, poor language, and persuasive content. Legitimate businesses provide verifiable contact details, while scam sites hide this information. Technical indicators: Kaspersky’s system analyzes domain name and age, IP address reputation, DNS configuration, HTTP security headers, and SSL certificate to detect suspicious resources. Regional threats: Kaspersky’s regional statistics reveal specific threats in different regions, such as fake online trading scam platforms in Africa and fake betting services in Latin America. Protection measures: Combining visual inspection, technical checks, and trusted online tools can help protect against financial loss and data theft. To stay safe, users should be cautious when visiting unfamiliar websites and look out for red flags such as strange domain names, unrealistic promises, and lack of contact information. It is also essential to use reputable security software and keep software up to date to protect against online threats.
There is nothing so useless as doing efficiently that which should not be done at all. Author: Peter Drucker
Source: Websites with an undefined trust level: avoiding the trap

content:
Authorities in Europe and North America have dismantled a criminal virtual private network (VPN) service known as First VPN, which was used by 25 ransomware groups to carry out attacks and other illicit activities. The service, promoted on Russian-speaking cybercrime forums, offered anonymous payments and a hidden infrastructure that enabled users to hide their identities. First VPN was taken down in an international operation that involved interviewing the service’s administrator, conducting a house search in Ukraine, and seizing infrastructure used to support cybercriminal activity globally.
The operation, which took place between May 19 and 20, resulted in the takedown of 33 servers and the seizure of several domains, including 1vpns[.]com, 1vpns[.]net, and 1vpns[.]org. The service had been active since 2014 and provided 32 exit node servers in 27 countries, including three in the US. First VPN offered various connection protocols and encryption options, including OpenConnect, WireGuard, and OpenVPN ECC, and accepted payments through Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies.
The FBI reported that First VPN’s website promoted itself as a tool for anonymity, promising not to cooperate with judicial authorities and not to store user data. However, the service’s FAQ noted that it prohibited the use of its servers for illicit activities, which was likely an attempt to escape liability. The takedown of First VPN is a significant blow to ransomware groups and other cybercriminals who relied on the service to carry out their activities. The international operation demonstrates the cooperation and coordination between law enforcement agencies in disrupting and dismantling criminal infrastructure.
The dismantling of First VPN highlights the importance of cooperation between law enforcement agencies and the private sector in combating cybercrime. The operation also underscores the need for individuals and organizations to be vigilant in their online activities and to use reputable and secure VPN services. The takedown of First VPN is a significant step in disrupting the activities of ransomware groups and other cybercriminals, and it is likely to have a lasting impact on the cybercrime landscape.
You are important enough to ask and you are blessed enough to receive back. Author: Wayne Dyer
Source: First VPN Dismantled in Global Takedown Over Use by 25 Ransomware Groups

content:
A cybersecurity breach has occurred at the US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after a contractor intentionally published sensitive information, including AWS GovCloud keys, on a public GitHub account. The leak, which was reported by KrebsOnSecurity, has raised concerns among lawmakers, with Senators and Representatives demanding answers from CISA.
The breach is believed to have occurred in November 2025, and the exposed information includes plaintext credentials to dozens of internal CISA systems. Experts have warned that the leak could provide a roadmap for adversaries to gain access to federal networks.
CISA has acknowledged the breach but claims that there is no indication that any sensitive data was compromised. However, lawmakers are skeptical, and Sen. Maggie Hassan (D-NH) has written to CISA’s Acting Director, Nick Andersen, expressing concerns about the agency’s internal policies and procedures.
Rep. Bennie Thompson (D-MS) has also written to Andersen, stating that the incident reflects a diminished security culture and/or an inability for CISA to adequately manage its contract support.
The breach is still being contained, and CISA is working to invalidate and replace the exposed keys and secrets. However, experts have warned that cybercrime groups or foreign adversaries may have already noticed the publication of the sensitive information and could be using it to gain access to federal networks.
The incident highlights the challenges of preventing insider threats and the importance of implementing robust security controls to prevent sensitive information from being leaked. Experts have noted that while technical controls can help prevent breaches, human error and intentional actions by employees or contractors can still pose a significant risk.
Key takeaways from the breach include:
The importance of implementing robust security controls to prevent sensitive information from being leaked The need for agencies to have clear policies and procedures in place to prevent insider threats The challenges of preventing intentional actions by employees or contractors The importance of monitoring public code platforms, such as GitHub, for exposed keys and credentials The potential for cybercrime groups or foreign adversaries to exploit leaked sensitive information to gain access to federal networks. You can never cross the ocean unless you have the courage to lose sight of the shore.
Source: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak

content:
A cybersecurity threat actor known as Ghostwriter, aligned with Belarus, has been targeting Ukrainian government entities with a phishing campaign using lures related to the Prometheus online learning platform. The campaign, active since spring 2026, involves sending phishing emails with a PDF attachment that leads to the download of a malicious JavaScript file called OYSTERFRESH. This file displays a decoy document while writing an obfuscated payload called OYSTERBLUES to the Windows Registry, which harvests system information and sends it to a command-and-control server. The final payload is believed to be Cobalt Strike, a framework used for post-exploitation activities.
To mitigate this threat, the Computer Emergency Response Team of Ukraine (CERT-UA) recommends restricting the ability to run wscript.exe for standard user accounts. This campaign is part of a larger effort by Russia-aligned threat actors to target Ukraine, using tactics such as social engineering, exploiting vulnerabilities, and using compromised accounts to gain access to sensitive information.
Additionally, a pro-Kremlin propaganda campaign has been uncovered, which hijacked real users’ accounts on the Bluesky platform to post fake content since 2024. The campaign, attributed to a Moscow-based company called Social Design Agency, has been linked to a larger effort known as Matryoshka. Bluesky has taken steps to suspend the hijacked accounts until the owners initiate a reset.
Key points:
Ghostwriter threat actor targets Ukrainian government entities with phishing campaign Campaign uses lures related to Prometheus online learning platform Malicious JavaScript file OYSTERFRESH displays decoy document while writing obfuscated payload to Windows Registry Final payload believed to be Cobalt Strike framework CERT-UA recommends restricting wscript.exe for standard user accounts to mitigate threat Campaign part of larger effort by Russia-aligned threat actors to target Ukraine Pro-Kremlin propaganda campaign hijacks Bluesky users’ accounts to post fake content. Ideals are an imaginative understanding of that which is desirable in that which is possible. Author: Walter Lippmann
Source: Ghostwriter Targets Ukraine Government Entities with Prometheus Phishing Malware

A contractor for CISA had a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and internal CISA systems. The repository included sensitive information on how CISA builds, tests, and deploys software internally. This leak is considered one of the most severe government data breaches in recent history.
The article is followed by a commentary criticizing the US government’s approach to hiring and appointments, suggesting that loyalty is prioritized over expertise and skill level, leading to unqualified individuals being placed in vital positions. Japanese cybersecurity software company Trend Micro has addressed an Apex One zero-day vulnerability exploited in attacks targeting Windows systems. Apex One is Trend Micro’s enterprise-grade endpoint security platform that protects corporate networks from a wide range of security threats, including malware, ransomware, fileless attacks, and web-based threats. Tracked as CVE-2026-34926, this directory traversal vulnerability in the Apex One (on-premises) server allows local attackers with admin privileges to inject malicious code. “A directory traversal vulnerability in the Apex One (on-premise) server could allow a pre-authenticated local attacker to modify a key table on the server to inject malicious code to deploy to agents on affected installations,” Trend Micro saidon Thursday. “This vulnerability is only exploitable on the on-premise version of Apex One and a potential attacker must have access to the Apex One Server and already obtained administrative credentials to the server

content:
Megalodon GitHub Attack: 5,561 Repositories Compromised with Malicious CI/CD Workflows
A new automated campaign, dubbed Megalodon, has targeted 5,561 GitHub repositories, injecting malicious GitHub Actions workflows that exfiltrate sensitive data, including CI secrets, cloud credentials, SSH keys, and source code secrets. The attack, which occurred within a six-hour window, pushed 5,718 malicious commits to the compromised repositories.
How the Attack Works:
The attacker uses throwaway GitHub accounts and forged author identities to inject malicious workflows into repositories. The workflows contain base64-encoded bash payloads that steal sensitive data and send it to a command and control (C2) server. The attacker targets CI/CD runners, using two payload variants: SysDiag and Optimize-Build. Stolen Data:
CI environment variables AWS credentials Google Cloud access tokens SSH private keys Docker and Kubernetes configurations Vault tokens Terraform credentials API keys Database connection strings JWTs PEM private keys Cloud tokens Impact:
The attack has compromised a large number of repositories, including @tiledesk/tiledesk-server. The malware executes inside CI/CD pipelines, allowing the theft of credentials and secrets at scale. The attack is part of a larger campaign by TeamPCP, which has compromised hundreds of open-source tools and is financially and geopolitically motivated. Related Attacks:
TeamPCP has also compromised npm packages, including a recent attack that used a throwaway account to publish nine malicious packages impersonating Polymarket trading CLI tools. The packages steal victims’ Ethereum/Polygon private keys via a postinstall hook. Mitigation:
npm has invalidated granular access tokens with write access that bypass two-factor authentication (2FA). Users are urged to switch to Trusted Publishing to reduce reliance on such tokens. Repository owners should be cautious when merging commits and should monitor their CI/CD pipelines for suspicious activity. We learn what we have said from those who listen to our speaking. Author: Kenneth Patton
Source: Megalodon GitHub Attack Targets 5,561 Repos with Malicious CI/CD Workflows

The article “Making Vulnerable Drivers Exploitable Without Hardware - The BYOVD Perspective” discusses how to interact with Windows kernel mode drivers from user mode without the hardware they were developed for. This is particularly relevant for BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) attacks, where attackers exploit vulnerabilities in drivers to disrupt system defenses.
The article provides a technical analysis of how drivers create and maintain device objects, which are the most viable attack vector. It identifies two common patterns:
Unconditional creation upon driver load: Some drivers create their device objects directly from within their DriverEntry function, making them easily accessible from user mode. Conditional device creation and maintenance: Other drivers perform additional checks, such as checking for specific hardware or registry keys, before creating their device objects. The article explores how to work around these checks and create device objects even in the absence of the required hardware. It focuses on PnP (Plug and Play) drivers, which make up most device drivers, and examines the role of PnP-specific callbacks, such as AddDevice and IRP_MJ_PNP handlers.
Key findings include:
Many drivers create device objects unconditionally upon driver load, making them easily exploitable. Conditional device creation and maintenance can be worked around by manipulating the driver’s internal state or by using PnP-specific callbacks. AddDevice is a critical function in PnP drivers, responsible for creating functional device objects and filter device objects. By invoking AddDevice, an attacker can create a device object and potentially exploit vulnerabilities in the driver. The article concludes that understanding how drivers create and maintain device objects is crucial for evaluating the exploitability of vulnerabilities in Windows kernel mode drivers. By applying the techniques described in the article, attackers can potentially exploit vulnerabilities in drivers even in the absence of the required hardware, making BYOVD attacks more feasible.
Overall, the article provides valuable insights into the inner workings of Windows kernel mode drivers and highlights the importance of considering the attack surface and exploitability of vulnerabilities in drivers, even in the absence of specific hardware.
I don’t believe in failure. It is not failure if you enjoyed the process. Author: Oprah Winfrey
Source: Making Vulnerable Drivers Exploitable Without Hardware - The BYOVD Perspective

content:
The article discusses the recent activities of the Cloud Atlas APT group, a cyber threat actor that has been active since 2014. The group has been observed using new tools and techniques to target government agencies and diplomatic entities in Russia and Belarus. The main findings of the article are:
Initial Infection: Cloud Atlas uses phishing emails with malicious attachments, such as ZIP archives containing LNK files, to gain initial access to victim systems. Malicious Payload: The LNK files execute PowerShell scripts that download and install additional malware, including the VBCloud and PowerShower backdoors. VBCloud Backdoor: This backdoor is designed to steal files with specific extensions (e.g., DOC, PDF, XLS) and exfiltrate them to the attackers’ command and control (C2) server. PowerShower Backdoor: This backdoor is used for network reconnaissance and lateral movement within the victim’s infrastructure. It can collect information about running processes, administrator groups, and domain controllers, and conduct “Kerberoasting” attacks to steal password hashes. Reverse SSH Tunneling: Cloud Atlas uses reverse SSH tunnels to maintain access to compromised systems and bypass firewall rules. Patched OpenSSH: The group uses modified OpenSSH binaries to evade detection and ensure stealth. RevSocks: Cloud Atlas also uses RevSocks, a tool written in Golang, to establish tunnels and proxy connections. Tor Tunneling: The group uses the Tor network to maintain control over compromised hosts and access them via RDP. New Tools: Cloud Atlas has developed new tools, such as PowerCloud, which collects user data with administrator privileges and writes it to Google Sheets in Base64 format. Victims: The group’s targets are primarily government agencies and diplomatic entities in Russia and Belarus. The article concludes that Cloud Atlas continues to evolve and expand its arsenal, using new tools and techniques to target its victims. The group’s activities are attributed to the Cloud Atlas APT group with a high degree of confidence, based on the use of similar techniques and tools in the past.
It isn’t where you come from, it’s where you’re going that counts. Author: Ella Fitzgerald
Source: Cloud Atlas activity in the second half of 2025 and early 2026: new tools and a new payload

content:
A 23-year-old Canadian man, Jacob Butler, has been arrested and charged with operating a distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) botnet called Kimwolf. The botnet, a variant of AISURU, infected devices such as digital photo frames and web cameras, and was used to launch DDoS attacks on computers and servers worldwide, including US Department of Defense networks. Butler allegedly sold access to the infected devices to other cybercriminals, who used them to launch attacks. The botnet is estimated to have issued over 25,000 attack commands and was involved in record-setting DDoS attacks. Butler faces up to 10 years in prison if convicted. The arrest is part of a larger law enforcement operation that disrupted the command-and-control infrastructure of several DDoS botnets, including Kimwolf, and seized online services supporting 45 DDoS-for-hire platforms.
Each misfortune you encounter will carry in it the seed of tomorrows good luck. Author: Og Mandino
Source: Kimwolf DDoS Botnet Operator Arrested in Canada Over DDoS-for-Hire Attacks

content:
The US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has added two vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog due to evidence of active exploitation. The vulnerabilities are:
CVE-2025-34291 (Langflow): An origin validation error that allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code and compromise the system (CVSS score: 9.4). This vulnerability has been exploited by an Iranian state-sponsored hacking group to gain initial access to target networks. CVE-2026-34926 (Trend Micro Apex One): A directory traversal vulnerability that allows a local attacker to inject malicious code into the server (CVSS score: 6.7). Trend Micro has observed at least one instance of attempted exploitation in the wild. As a result, Federal Civilian Executive Branch (FCEB) agencies are required to apply the necessary fixes by June 4, 2026, to secure their networks. The vulnerabilities can lead to severe consequences, including full system compromise, exposure of sensitive access tokens and API keys, and cascading compromise across integrated downstream services.
It takes courage to grow up and become who you really are. Author: E. E. Cummings
Source: CISA Adds Exploited Langflow and Trend Micro Apex One Vulnerabilities to KEV

content:
Cisco Patches Critical Security Flaw in Secure Workload
Cisco has released updates to fix a maximum-severity security vulnerability (CVE-2026-20223, CVSS score: 10.0) in its Secure Workload product. The flaw, which affects both SaaS and on-prem deployments, allows an unauthenticated, remote attacker to access sensitive data by sending a crafted API request. A successful exploit could enable the attacker to read sensitive information and make configuration changes with the privileges of the Site Admin user.
Key Points:
The vulnerability is due to insufficient validation and authentication in REST API endpoints. There are no workarounds to address the issue. The flaw affects Cisco Secure Workload Cluster Software, regardless of device configuration. Cisco has released fixed versions: 3.10.8.3, 4.0.3.17, and recommends migrating from Release 3.9 and earlier. The vulnerability was discovered during internal security testing, and there is no evidence of it being exploited in the wild. Context:
This disclosure comes a week after Cisco revealed another maximum-severity authentication bypass flaw (CVE-2026-20182) in its Catalyst SD-WAN Controller, which has been exploited by a threat actor. Users are advised to update their Secure Workload software to the latest fixed versions to prevent potential attacks.
The greatest way to live with honour in this world is to be what we pretend to be. Author: Socrates
Source: Cisco Patches CVSS 10.0 Secure Workload REST API Flaw Enabling Data Access

content:
A 23-year-old Canadian man, Jacob Butler, also known as “Dort”, has been arrested and charged in both the US and Canada for allegedly building and operating the Kimwolf botnet. The botnet, which infected millions of devices, was used to launch massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. The attacks targeted various organizations, including the US Department of Defense, and resulted in financial losses exceeding $1 million for some victims.
Butler was identified as the alleged botmaster by cybersecurity researcher Brian Krebs, who had been targeted by Dort in a series of DDoS, doxing, and swatting campaigns. The investigation involved the analysis of IP addresses, online account information, transaction records, and online messaging application records.
Butler faces charges in Canada, including unauthorized use of a computer, possession of a device to obtain unauthorized use of a computer system, and mischief in relation to computer data. In the US, he faces one count of aiding and abetting computer intrusion, which carries a maximum sentence of 10 years in prison.
The case highlights the growing threat of IoT botnets and the importance of international cooperation in combating cybercrime. The arrest of Butler is seen as a significant development in the fight against DDoS attacks and other forms of cybercrime.
The article also sparks a discussion about the severity of the sentence that Butler should face, with some commenters arguing that he should receive a harsher sentence due to the scale of the attacks and the potential harm caused. Others raise concerns about the potential leniency of the sentence, given Butler’s age and lack of prior criminal history.
In order to win, you must expect to win. Author: Richard Bach
Source: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada

content:
The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) has settled with three marketing companies - Cox Media Group, MindSift LLC, and 1010 Digital Works - over allegations of deceptive advertising practices. The companies claimed to offer a service called “Active Listening” that could target ads based on audio recordings collected from consumers’ smart devices. However, the FTC found that this service did not actually work as advertised. The companies claimed to use AI to target ads based on conversations collected from devices such as smartphones, smart TVs, and smart speakers, and also claimed that consumers had consented to the collection and use of their voice data.
In reality, the FTC alleges that the companies were simply reselling consumer email lists at a significant markup. As part of the settlement, the companies have agreed to pay a total of $930,000, with Cox Media Group paying $880,000 and the other two companies paying $25,000 each. The settlement also requires the companies to stop making misrepresentations about their marketing services and the collection and use of audio recordings. The FTC emphasized the importance of honesty in business practices, stating that companies must be truthful with their customers.
The smallest flower is a thought, a life answering to some feature of the Great Whole, of whom they have a persistent intuition. Author: Honore de Balzac
Source: ‘Creepy’ Listening Tool for Targeted Ads Didn’t Actually Work, FTC Says

content:
Summary of Showboat Linux Malware Article
Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a new Linux malware called Showboat, which has been used to target a telecommunications provider in the Middle East since mid-2022. The malware is a modular post-exploitation framework that can spawn a remote shell, transfer files, and function as a SOCKS5 proxy. It is believed to have been employed by threat actors affiliated with China, including the Calypso group, which has been active since 2016 and has targeted state institutions in several countries.
Key Features of Showboat Malware:
Modular design: Showboat is a modular framework that can be customized to perform various tasks. Remote shell access: The malware can spawn a remote shell, allowing attackers to interact with the compromised system. File transfer: Showboat can transfer files to and from the compromised system. SOCKS5 proxy: The malware can function as a SOCKS5 proxy, allowing attackers to access devices that are not exposed to the internet. Rootkit-like capabilities: Showboat has rootkit-like capabilities, making it difficult to detect and remove. Attribution and Connections:
Calypso group: The malware is believed to have been employed by the Calypso group, which is affiliated with China. China-linked groups: Showboat is part of a broader cluster of malware used by multiple China-linked groups, including PlugX, ShadowPad, and NosyDoor. Resource pooling: The use of Showboat and other shared frameworks suggests the presence of a digital quartermaster that supplies state-sponsored threat actors with necessary tooling. Attack Chain and Victims:
Initial access vector: The exact initial access vector used to deliver the malware is unknown. Victims: Two victims have been identified: an Afghanistan-based internet service provider (ISP) and another unknown entity located in Azerbaijan. Secondary C2 cluster: A secondary C2 cluster has been discovered, which may indicate additional compromises in the U.S. and Ukraine. Conclusion:
The discovery of Showboat malware highlights the ongoing threat of state-sponsored cyberattacks and the importance of monitoring and detecting suspicious activity. The use of shared frameworks and resource pooling by China-linked groups suggests a high level of coordination and cooperation among these actors.
If you have no respect for your own values how can you be worthy of respect from others. Author: Byron Pulsifer
Source: Showboat Linux Malware Hits Middle East Telecom with SOCKS5 Proxy Backdoor

Zero-day exploits: The Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 hacking contest revealed 47 zero-day flaws in various products, including Windows, Linux, VMware, and NVIDIA. The contest awarded $1,298,250 in rewards to security researchers. Agentic AI security warning: The UK National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) warned organizations to implement adequate security controls when using agentic artificial intelligence (AI) tools, as they can pose a significant risk if not properly designed or privileged. Signal alternative: The Polish government is urging public officials to stop using Signal and instead use an encrypted messenger called mSzyfr, citing social engineering attacks orchestrated by advanced persistent threat (APT) groups. Fraud suspects unmasked: The Dutch police have unmasked 74 out of 100 suspected fraudsters through an initiative called Game Over?! that displays blurred photos of the suspects on billboards and online advertisements. Espionage admission: US President Donald Trump admitted that the US and China engage in cyber espionage activities, including spying on each other. Ransomware attack: A ransomware family known as Gunra has targeted five South Korean companies, with the group claiming 32 victims as of March 2026. Composer token leak: Composer, a dependency manager for PHP, has fixed a vulnerability that leaked GitHub tokens, which could be used to access sensitive information. Linux rootkit: A Linux malware named OrBit has been found to be actively refined and maintained by its operators, with new artifacts indicating its continued use by cybercrime groups. AI-driven intrusions: Two emerging campaigns, SHADOW-AETHER-040 and SHADOW-AETHER-064, have used agentic AI to facilitate intrusion operations against governments and financial organizations in Latin America. Mythos intel sharing: Anthropic has begun allowing users of its Mythos AI model to share cybersecurity threats with others, expanding its intel sharing capabilities. Discord encryption: Discord has announced that all voice and video calls on its platform are now protected by default with end-to-end encryption (E2EE). Azure identities abused: Microsoft has revealed a sophisticated attack by Storm-2949 that abused Azure identities to exfiltrate sensitive data from an organization’s high-value assets. content:
Microsoft has warned of two actively exploited vulnerabilities in its Defender software. The first vulnerability, CVE-2026-41091, is a privilege escalation flaw that could allow an attacker to gain SYSTEM privileges, and has a CVSS score of 7.8. The second vulnerability, CVE-2026-45498, is a denial-of-service bug with a CVSS score of 4.0. Both vulnerabilities have been addressed in recent updates to Microsoft Defender Antimalware Platform.
Microsoft has credited five parties with discovering and reporting the flaws, and the US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has added both vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, requiring federal agencies to apply the fixes by June 3, 2026.
To ensure protection, users are advised to check for updates in the Windows Security program and verify that the latest version of the Microsoft Malware Protection Platform and definition updates are being downloaded and installed.
Additionally, CISA has added four other Microsoft flaws from 2008, 2009, and 2010 to the KEV catalog, as well as a vulnerability in Adobe Acrobat and Reader. This is the third Microsoft vulnerability to be flagged as exploited within a week, following a cross-site scripting flaw in Exchange Server last week.
Be glad of life because it gives you the chance to love, to work, to play, and to look up at the stars. Author: Henry Van Dyke
Source: Microsoft Warns of Two Actively Exploited Defender Vulnerabilities

content:
A hacker group known as TeamPCP has been carrying out a massive series of software supply chain attacks, corrupting hundreds of open-source tools and extorting victims for profit. The group has breached numerous companies, including GitHub, Anthropic, and Mercor, and has accessed thousands of code repositories. TeamPCP’s tactics involve gaining access to a network where an open-source tool is being developed, planting malware in the tool, and then using stolen credentials to publish malicious versions of other software development tools.
The group has automated many of its attacks using a self-spreading worm called Mini Shai-Hulud, which creates encrypted credentials stolen from victims and leaves a message referencing the sci-fi novel Dune. TeamPCP appears to be financially motivated and often deploys ransomware or data extortion campaigns against its targets.
The scale of TeamPCP’s attacks has expanded dramatically in recent months, with the group hacking more software utilities and leading to a cascading effect of supply chain attacks. The fallout has been severe, with breaches of the European Commission’s public website, source code of Anthropic’s Claude, and many other organizations.
To protect themselves, organizations can practice security “hygiene” by carefully managing authentication tokens, imposing access restrictions, and rotating credentials regularly. Experts also recommend safeguards such as “age-gating” updates to open-source tools, vetting and installing security updates, and holding off on immediate updates to code that’s been newly published and may be malicious.
The attacks raise hard questions about how to safely use open-source software in an era of mounting supply chain attacks. Experts recommend taking trust-but-verify measures, such as analyzing updates for malware before rolling them out across a network, and implementing a “cool-down” period before downloading and running code.
Key takeaways:
TeamPCP has carried out a massive series of software supply chain attacks, corrupting hundreds of open-source tools and extorting victims for profit. The group’s tactics involve gaining access to a network where an open-source tool is being developed, planting malware in the tool, and then using stolen credentials to publish malicious versions of other software development tools. TeamPCP appears to be financially motivated and often deploys ransomware or data extortion campaigns against its targets. Organizations can protect themselves by practicing security “hygiene”, rotating credentials regularly, and implementing safeguards such as “age-gating” updates to open-source tools. The attacks raise hard questions about how to safely use open-source software in an era of mounting supply chain Though no one can go back and make a brand new start, anyone can start from now and make a brand new ending.
Source: A Hacker Group Is Poisoning Open Source Code at an Unprecedented Scale

content:
9-Year-Old Linux Kernel Vulnerability Discovered
A cybersecurity research team has identified a 9-year-old flaw in the Linux kernel, tracked as CVE-2026-46333, which allows an unprivileged local user to execute arbitrary commands as root on major Linux distributions such as Debian, Fedora, and Ubuntu. The vulnerability, also known as “ssh-keysign-pwn,” is caused by improper privilege management in the kernel’s __ptrace_may_access() function, introduced in November 2016.
Exploitation and Impact
The vulnerability can be exploited to disclose sensitive files, including /etc/shadow and host private keys, and execute arbitrary commands as root through four different exploits. Successful exploitation could allow a local attacker to gain root privileges, potentially leading to a complete system compromise.
Recommendations and Mitigations
To mitigate the vulnerability, users are advised to apply the latest kernel update released by their Linux distribution. If updates cannot be applied immediately, a temporary workaround is to raise the kernel.yama.ptrace_scope value to 2. Additionally, users who have allowed untrusted local users during the exposure window should rotate their host keys and review any administrative material that may have been compromised.
Related Vulnerability: PinTheft
A separate local privilege escalation flaw, called PinTheft, has also been discovered, which allows local attackers to gain root privileges on Arch Linux systems. The exploit requires specific conditions to be met, including the presence of the Reliable Datagram Sockets (RDS) module and io_ring.
Key Takeaways
A 9-year-old Linux kernel vulnerability (CVE-2026-46333) has been discovered, allowing unprivileged local users to execute arbitrary commands as root. The vulnerability affects major Linux distributions, including Debian, Fedora, and Ubuntu. Users should apply the latest kernel update or use temporary workarounds to mitigate the vulnerability. A separate local privilege escalation flaw, PinTheft, has been discovered, affecting Arch Linux systems. It’s so simple to be wise. Just think of something stupid to say and then don’t say it. Author: Sam Levenson
Source: 9-Year-Old Linux Kernel Flaw Enables Root Command Execution on Major Distros

content:
The article discusses how the European Union, particularly France, is accelerating its efforts to reduce its dependence on US technology due to concerns over data security, the Trump administration’s unpredictability, and changing prices. The French government is developing and deploying its own technology for government officials, including a video-calling platform called Visio, and a set of productivity tools called “LaSuite”. The goal is to shift to French, European, and open-source technology alternatives, with data being stored locally in the country.
The article highlights that:
Over 40,000 French government staff have started using the home-grown video platform Visio, and the rest will move away from Zoom, Microsoft Teams, and others by 2027. The French government is working with other countries and private firms on the development of its tools, and is aiming to lead by example in reducing its reliance on US tech. Other European countries, such as the Netherlands, Austria, Belgium, Denmark, and Finland, are also making efforts to reduce their dependence on US technology. The French efforts are not limited to the central government, with cities like Lyon also moving away from American technology and towards open-source alternatives. The article cites experts who say that while it’s not possible to completely eliminate US tech from European systems, open-source technology and digital public infrastructure could be a way to reduce dependence on US companies. The article also mentions that the EU’s dependence on US tech companies is significant, with:
70% of the EU market using Google, Microsoft, and Amazon’s cloud services. 80% of European company software spending going to US firms. At least 23 countries depending on US tech firms for “critical national security functions”. Overall, the article suggests that the EU, particularly France, is taking steps to reduce its reliance on US technology and promote digital sovereignty, but acknowledges that it’s a complex and challenging process.
By going beyond your own problems and taking care of others, you gain inner strength, self-confidence, courage, and a greater sense of calm. Author: Dalai Lama
Source: The EU Is Going Through a Trump-Fueled Breakup With Big Tech

content:
GitHub Internal Repositories Breach
GitHub has confirmed a breach of its internal repositories due to a malicious version of the Nx Console VS Code extension. The extension, nrwl.angular-console, was compromised after one of its developers’ systems was hacked in the wake of the recent TanStack supply chain attack. The breach allowed the threat actor, TeamPCP, to exfiltrate approximately 3,800 repositories.
Key Points:
Malicious Extension: The poisoned extension was live on Visual Studio Marketplace for only 18 minutes, but it was enough to distribute a credential stealer capable of harvesting sensitive data from various sources. Impact: GitHub has no evidence of impact to customer information stored outside of its internal repositories, but some internal repositories may contain customer information, such as support interactions. Response: GitHub has taken steps to contain the incident, rotated critical secrets, and is continuing to monitor the situation for follow-on activity. Threat Actor: TeamPCP, a cybercriminal group, has gained notoriety for large-scale software supply chain attacks, targeting widely-used open-source projects and security-adjacent tools. Security Concerns: The incident highlights the need for deeper, more fundamental changes to how developer tooling and open source distribution are secured, as well as the risks associated with auto-update features in extension marketplaces. Recommendations:
Developer Awareness: Developers should be cautious when installing and updating extensions, and consider disabling auto-update features to prevent potential security risks. Marketplace Security: Extension marketplaces should impose review gates and waiting periods between updates to prevent malicious publishers from pushing compromised code to users. Collaboration: Open-source maintainers and security experts should work together to address the deeper structural problems around software supply chain security. If you look into your own heart, and you find nothing wrong there, what is there to worry about? What is there to fear? Author: Confucius
Source: GitHub Internal Repositories Breached via Malicious Nx Console VS Code Extension

Key Points:

content:
A highly critical security vulnerability (CVE-2026-9082) has been discovered in Drupal Core, affecting sites that use PostgreSQL databases. The vulnerability, with a CVSS score of 6.5, allows attackers to send specially crafted requests, leading to arbitrary SQL injection, information disclosure, privilege escalation, and remote code execution (RCE) attacks.
The flaw can be exploited by anonymous users and is fixed in the following Drupal versions:
Drupal 11.3.10 Drupal 11.2.12 Drupal 11.1.10 Drupal 10.6.9 Drupal 10.5.10 Drupal 10.4.10 Drupal 7 is not affected. Manual patches are also available for end-of-life versions 9 and 8. Users of unsupported versions (Drupal 11.1.x, 11.0.x, 10.4.x, and below, as well as Drupal 8 and 9) are advised to upgrade to a supported version as they do not receive security coverage and may have other unpatched vulnerabilities.
Money was never a big motivation for me, except as a way to keep score. The real excitement is playing the game. Author: Donald Trump
